subsystem: ImageMagick MagickCore EXIF metadata property accessor. The single function GetEXIFProperty() decodes the TIFF/EXIF byte stream embedded in an image file's 'exif' profile: it walks the EXIF Image File Directory (IFD) tree, reads directory entries, extracts tag values into a string, and registers them as image properties (e.g. 'exif:Model'). It processes attacker-controlled image metadata.
provenance: Identified by the MagickCore API surface and naming: the signature GetEXIFProperty(const Image*, const char*, ExceptionInfo*), the MagickBooleanType/MagickFalse/MagickTrue booleans, MagickPathExtent buffer sizing, the GetImageProfile/GetStringInfoDatum/AcquireQuantumMemory/AcquireString/FormatLocaleString/SplayTree helpers, the ReadPropertyByte/ReadPropertyUnsignedShort/ReadPropertySignedLong wire readers, and the EXIFTag[] table citing the CIPA DC-008 EXIF spec. The header comment 'sample: primevul_c_train_2286_fixed' marks it as a post-fix revision of a PrimeVul sample.
trust_boundary: Two distinct producers. (1) The 'property' argument is the caller/application query string (e.g. 'exif:Model', 'exif:*'); it is semi-trusted API input, not image data. (2) The bytes returned by GetImageProfile(image,"exif") are the raw EXIF/TIFF blob lifted verbatim from the image file and are FULLY attacker-controlled and hostile: the byte-order marker ('II'/'MM'), the 0x002a magic, the first-IFD offset, every IFD's entry count, and every 12-byte directory entry's tag/format/component-count/value-or-offset are chosen by whoever crafted the image. All pointer arithmetic (directory, q, p, p1, dir_offset, tag_offset1) is driven by these hostile fields, so every dereference depends on the parser's bounds checks against [exif, exif+length).

what_the_code_does.code:
static MagickBooleanType GetEXIFProperty(const Image *image,
  const char *property,ExceptionInfo *exception)
{
#define MaxDirectoryStack  16
#define EXIF_DELIMITER  "\n"
#define EXIF_NUM_FORMATS  12
#define EXIF_FMT_BYTE  1
#define EXIF_FMT_STRING  2
#define EXIF_FMT_USHORT  3
#define EXIF_FMT_ULONG  4
#define EXIF_FMT_URATIONAL  5
#define EXIF_FMT_SBYTE  6
#define EXIF_FMT_UNDEFINED  7
#define EXIF_FMT_SSHORT  8
#define EXIF_FMT_SLONG  9
#define EXIF_FMT_SRATIONAL  10
#define EXIF_FMT_SINGLE  11
#define EXIF_FMT_DOUBLE  12
#define TAG_EXIF_OFFSET  0x8769
#define TAG_GPS_OFFSET  0x8825
#define TAG_INTEROP_OFFSET  0xa005
what_the_code_does.explanation: The function returns MagickBooleanType and takes the Image, the caller's property query string, and an ExceptionInfo sink. The block of #defines establishes the parser's constants: MaxDirectoryStack (16) bounds the IFD-recursion stack used later; EXIF_NUM_FORMATS (12) is the number of TIFF field types; the EXIF_FMT_* values (1..12) are the TIFF format-type codes that appear in each directory entry and index the value-extraction switch and the tag_bytes[] size table. TAG_EXIF_OFFSET (0x8769), TAG_GPS_OFFSET (0x8825), and TAG_INTEROP_OFFSET (0xa005) are the special tag ids whose value is itself a pointer to a nested sub-IFD, which drives the recursive directory descent at the end of the loop.

what_the_code_does.code:
#define EXIFMultipleValues(size,format,arg) \
{ \
   ssize_t \
     component; \
 \
   size_t \
     len; \
 \
   unsigned char \
     *p1; \
 \
   len=0; \
   p1=p; \
   for (component=0; component < components; component++) \
   { \
     len+=FormatLocaleString(buffer+len,MagickPathExtent-len,format", ",arg); \
     if (len >= (MagickPathExtent-1)) \
       len=MagickPathExtent-1; \
     p1+=size; \
   } \
   if (len > 1) \
     buffer[len-2]='\0'; \
   value=AcquireString(buffer); \
}
what_the_code_does.explanation: EXIFMultipleValues is a code-generating macro used by every scalar numeric format case. Given a per-element byte 'size', a printf 'format', and an 'arg' expression that reads from the pointer p1, it starts p1 at the entry's value pointer p, then loops 'components' times (components is the attacker-supplied element count from the directory entry). Each iteration appends 'arg' followed by ', ' into the shared stack buffer via FormatLocaleString, whose second argument MagickPathExtent-len bounds the write so the destination buffer cannot overflow. It clamps len to MagickPathExtent-1 to prevent len running past the buffer, advances the READ pointer p1 by 'size' bytes, strips the trailing ', ' by writing buffer[len-2]='\0', and finally copies the result into 'value' via AcquireString. Crucially the loop's read side (arg dereferences p1) has NO bounds check of its own against exif+length.

what_the_code_does.code:
#define EXIFMultipleFractions(size,format,arg1,arg2) \
{ \
   ssize_t \
     component; \
 \
   size_t \
     len; \
 \
   unsigned char \
     *p1; \
 \
   len=0; \
   p1=p; \
   for (component=0; component < components; component++) \
   { \
     len+=FormatLocaleString(buffer+len,MagickPathExtent-len,format", ", \
       (arg1),(arg2)); \
     if (len >= (MagickPathExtent-1)) \
       len=MagickPathExtent-1; \
     p1+=size; \
   } \
   if (len > 1) \
     buffer[len-2]='\0'; \
   value=AcquireString(buffer); \
}
what_the_code_does.explanation: EXIFMultipleFractions is the two-argument sibling of the previous macro, used for the RATIONAL formats that store a numerator/denominator pair. It is structurally identical: p1 starts at p, the loop runs 'components' times reading two sub-values (arg1 from p1, arg2 typically from p1+4) per iteration, the FormatLocaleString write is bounded by MagickPathExtent-len, and p1 advances by 'size' (8 for rationals). As with EXIFMultipleValues, the WRITE into buffer is safe but the READS via p1 across the loop rely entirely on the caller having bounded p and components/number_bytes; the macro itself never re-checks p1 against exif+length.

DirectoryInfo is the per-frame record of the IFD-descent stack. 'directory' is a pointer into the EXIF blob at the start of an IFD; 'entry' is the next directory-entry index to resume at when the frame is popped; 'offset' is the tag_offset base (0 for normal IFDs, 0x10000 for GPS) added to tag ids so GPS tags do not collide with normal tags. An array of these forms the explicit stack that replaces true recursion.

TagInfo maps a numeric EXIF tag id to a human-readable name. 'tag' is the 16/17-bit tag number; 'description' is a FIXED 36-byte inline character array holding the 'exif:Name' string. Because the array is inline and fixed at 36 bytes, every string literal used to initialize it must fit in 35 characters plus a NUL. The description is compared against the caller's query with LocaleCompare and later copied out with FormatLocaleString, both of which assume it is NUL-terminated.

EXIFTag[] is a large static const lookup table of TagInfo entries (lines 99-395) mapping every known EXIF/GPS/thumbnail tag id to its 'exif:...' name. It is read-only program data, used both to resolve a textual query like 'exif:Model' to a numeric tag (default case of the selection switch) and to label tags when dumping all properties. GPS tags are stored with the 0x10000-based ids to keep them distinct from the base IFD.

The table is terminated by a sentinel entry whose tag is 0 and description is the empty string. The lookup loops (lines 514-523 and 764-773) stop when EXIFTag[i].tag == 0, so the sentinel is the loop terminator. The comment cites the CIPA DC-008 EXIF specification, confirming this is a standards-based EXIF decoder.

The locals: 'profile' is the StringInfo holding the EXIF blob; 'exif' is the base pointer to its bytes and 'directory' the current IFD pointer; 'directory_stack[MaxDirectoryStack]' is the 16-deep IFD stack; 'endian' records the detected byte order; 'length' is the blob size; 'number_entries' is the current IFD's entry count; 'tag' is the resolved query tag and 'tag_value' the tag read from an entry. 'exif_resources' is a SplayTree used to detect already-visited directory entries (loop protection). 'level' indexes the stack. tag_bytes[] is a 13-element table giving the byte width of each TIFF format code (index 0 unused; e.g. tag_bytes[EXIF_FMT_DOUBLE=12]=8); it is indexed by the attacker-supplied 'format' and multiplied by 'components' to size a value.

The function fetches the 'exif' profile and bails if absent, then validates the caller's property string is non-NULL and non-empty, skips leading whitespace, and requires strlen(property) > 5. The length gate guarantees property[0..5] are valid characters (plus a NUL at index >=6), so the subsequent read of property[5] and the property+=6 skip are in-bounds for the query string.

Tag selection based on property[5] (the character after the 'exif:' prefix). '*' sets all=1 (dump every tag, description=value form); '!' sets all=2 (tagid=value form); '#'/'@' parse the following 4 characters as a hex tag id (@ sets bit 0x10000 for the GPS namespace) after advancing property+=6 and requiring exactly 4 remaining hex digits; the default case linearly scans EXIFTag[] with LocaleCompare to resolve a symbolic name to a numeric tag. tag stays ~0UL (sentinel) if nothing matched. The hex parser reads exactly 4 characters bounded by the strlen(property)==4 check, so it does not over-read the query string.

After confirming a tag was resolved, it grabs the blob length and base pointer. ReadPropertyByte consumes one byte and decrements length, so the loop scans for the ASCII 'Exif\0\0' signature (0x45 0x78 0x69 0x66 0x00 0x00), which each byte-read shortens length past. It then requires at least 16 remaining bytes for the TIFF header. It reads the 2-byte byte-order marker: 0x4949 ('II') selects little-endian, 0x4D4D ('MM') big-endian, anything else is rejected. The endianness governs every subsequent multi-byte read.

It validates the TIFF magic 0x002a at exif+2, then reads the 4-byte first-IFD offset at exif+4 and rejects it if negative or >= length. directory is set to exif+offset (attacker-influenced but range-checked here and re-checked in the loop). It initializes the stack level, resume entry, and tag_offset base to zero, sets status pessimistically to MagickFalse, and creates a SplayTree (exif_resources) that will record visited entry pointers to break parsing cycles.

The main do-while pops the next IFD frame off the stack (restoring directory/entry/tag_offset), then guards the IFD pointer: it must lie within [exif, exif+length-2] so the following 2-byte read is in-bounds. number_entries is read as an unsigned 16-bit count (max 65535) from the IFD header. This directory bounds check at line 590 is the guard that keeps every attacker-provided stack directory pointer inside the buffer before it is dereferenced.

For each of the number_entries 12-byte directory entries, q points at the entry (2-byte IFD count header + 12*entry). q is bounded so all 12 entry bytes are readable (q <= exif+length-12). The SplayTree check aborts if this exact entry pointer was already processed, breaking parse cycles. It reads the 2-byte tag (offset by tag_offset), the 2-byte format code, and validates format is within [1, 12] (rejecting 0 and out-of-range so tag_bytes[format] is a valid index). It reads the 4-byte signed component count, rejects negatives, computes number_bytes = components*tag_bytes[format], and guards the multiplication with 'if (number_bytes < components) break' to catch integer overflow of the product.

This is the TIFF value-vs-offset rule: if the value fits in 4 bytes it is stored inline at q+8, so p=q+8 (and q+8..q+12 is within the entry, hence in-bounds). Otherwise the entry holds a 4-byte offset to the value elsewhere in the blob: dir_offset is read, rejected if negative or >= length, checked for dir_offset+number_bytes wraparound and for exceeding length, then p=exif+dir_offset. These checks establish that in the offset branch, p through p+number_bytes lies inside [exif, exif+length], which is the invariant the value-reading macros depend on.

If the caller asked for all tags or this entry's tag matches the requested tag, the value is extracted. A MagickPathExtent-sized stack buffer receives the formatted output. Before reading, p is bounds-checked: p must be >= exif and <= exif+length-tag_bytes[format], i.e. at least ONE element (tag_bytes[format] bytes) is readable at p. Note this check validates only a single element's worth of bytes, not the full number_bytes span that the multi-value loop will read.

The format switch extracts the value. BYTE/UNDEFINED allocate number_bytes+1 and copy each byte, substituting '.' for non-printable bytes (reading p[0..number_bytes-1]). SBYTE/SSHORT/USHORT/ULONG/SLONG/SINGLE/DOUBLE invoke EXIFMultipleValues with the matching element size, and URATIONAL/SRATIONAL invoke EXIFMultipleFractions — all looping 'components' times over p1. The STRING/default case (728) re-validates p against exif+length-number_bytes (the FULL span, unlike the single-element check at 652) before copying. The value ends NUL-terminated in every branch.

If a value string was produced, a key is built from the property (or, for all==1, from the EXIFTag[] description matching tag_value; for all==2, from a formatted '#%04lx'/'@%04lx' hex id). If level==2 (a thumbnail sub-IFD), the key prefix is rewritten from 'exif:' to 'exif:thumbnail:'. The property is registered via SetImageProperty unless already present, value and key are freed, and status is set MagickTrue. The description lookup for all==1 relies on EXIFTag[i].description being a NUL-terminated string.

This block (outside the tag-match block, so it runs for every entry) handles the sub-IFD pointer tags. If the entry's tag is the EXIF, Interop, or GPS offset tag, the 4-byte value at p is read as tag_offset1 (a pointer to a nested IFD). Guarded by tag_offset1 < length (a negative value cast to size_t becomes huge and fails) and level < MaxDirectoryStack-2 (=14), it saves the current frame, pushes the sub-IFD (exif+tag_offset1) onto directory_stack with a GPS tag_offset base of 0x10000 when applicable, and increments level twice. A duplicate-directory scan (831-835) breaks cycles, and it also reads the 'next IFD' link at directory+2+12*number_entries (guarded at 840 to keep the read in-bounds) to chain sibling directories. The do-while continues while level > 0, and finally the SplayTree is destroyed and status returned.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
for (component=0; component < components; component++) \
   { \
     len+=FormatLocaleString(buffer+len,MagickPathExtent-len,format", ",arg); \
     if (len >= (MagickPathExtent-1)) \
       len=MagickPathExtent-1; \
     p1+=size; \
   } \
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The EXIFMultipleValues / EXIFMultipleFractions loop reads 'components' elements from p1 (arg dereferences p1; fractions read p1 and p1+4) but performs NO bounds check on p1 against exif+length inside the loop. The write side is safe (FormatLocaleString is capped by MagickPathExtent-len), but the READ side is bounded only externally: it depends on the single-element check at line 652 PLUS the number_bytes derivation (inline number_bytes<=4 at 627, or dir_offset+number_bytes<=length at 642) guaranteeing p+components*size <= exif+length. Because size == tag_bytes[format] for every case, in this fixed revision that invariant holds and the reads stay in-bounds. But the safety is entirely non-local to the macro: if any caller ever invokes it with a 'size' that differs from tag_bytes[format], or the 627/642 span checks are weakened, the loop over-reads the heap (CWE-125). The invariant the implementation must uphold: p1 + components*size must never exceed exif+length, which currently requires size==tag_bytes[format] and the number_bytes span check to both remain intact.

This pre-extraction guard validates only ONE element's worth of bytes at p (tag_bytes[format]), not the full number_bytes span that the multi-value loop consumes. For components > 1 the loop reads number_bytes = components*tag_bytes[format] bytes, far beyond what this check validates. In the current code the over-read is prevented not by this line but by the number_bytes span checks at 627/642, so this check is insufficient on its own. Contrast with the STRING branch at line 731 which correctly re-checks p against exif+length-number_bytes. Invariant: the value must be bounded against the FULL number_bytes it will read, not one element; this line silently trusts the earlier span logic and would not catch a regression there (CWE-125).

directory_stack is a fixed 16-element array (MaxDirectoryStack) indexed by 'level', which is a bare counter with no independent bound. The push path enters only when level < MaxDirectoryStack-2 (=14, line 816) but then writes directory_stack[level] and increments level up to THREE times (827, 839, and conditionally 850). Tracing it: entering with level<=13, the writes land at indices <=13, then <=14, and the inner push at 847-850 is re-guarded by level < 14 so it cannot fire once level has reached 14/15; the maximum index written is 14, which is valid for a [16] array. So no overflow occurs in this revision. However, this is a fixed-size buffer driven by attacker-triggered pushes (each EXIF/GPS/Interop offset tag pushes frames), and the correctness hinges on the exact interaction of the 'level < MaxDirectoryStack-2' guards with the multiple level++ steps. Invariant: level must remain in [0,15] across every write; if the guard bound or the number of increments is ever changed (e.g. to MaxDirectoryStack-1), directory_stack[level] becomes an out-of-bounds stack write (CWE-787). Confirm the guard/increment accounting stays consistent.

TagInfo.description is a fixed 36-byte inline buffer, and the longest initializer literal ('exif:ThumbnailPrimaryChromaticities', line 227) is exactly 35 characters + 1 NUL = 36 bytes, leaving ZERO headroom. The array is consumed as a NUL-terminated C string by LocaleCompare (line 518) when resolving a symbolic query and by FormatLocaleString with '%s' (line 774) when dumping tag names. If any future tag name of 36+ characters is added to the table, the string literal initializer silently drops the terminating NUL (C permits initializing a char[N] from an exactly-N-char literal without the NUL), turning every subsequent LocaleCompare/printf of that entry into an out-of-bounds read past the struct (CWE-125). Invariant: every EXIFTag[] description must be <= 35 characters so the NUL fits; this is currently true but exactly saturated, a maintenance hazard.

The sub-IFD pointer is read with ReadPropertySignedLong(endian,p), a 4-byte read at p, executed for EVERY entry whose tag is an offset tag REGARDLESS of the entry's format field, and independently of the value-extraction path. If the tag matched neither 'all' nor the requested tag, the single-element bound check at line 652 never runs. Reachability analysis shows p is still safe here: for the inline branch p=q+8 with q<=exif+length-12 gives p+4<=exif+length, and for the offset branch number_bytes>4 so p+4<=p+number_bytes<=exif+length. So the 4-byte read is in-bounds in this revision. The residual risk is the ReadProperty* helpers take only a raw pointer and no length (see below): the bound is established entirely by the pointer arithmetic that precedes each call, so any future change to how p is computed for offset tags could turn this into an out-of-bounds read (CWE-125). Invariant: p must have 4 readable bytes whenever an offset tag is processed.

Every ReadPropertyByte/ReadPropertySignedShort/ReadPropertyUnsignedShort/ReadPropertySignedLong/ReadPropertyUnsignedLong call takes a pointer (and for the byte reader a length) but the multi-byte readers receive NO length and cannot self-limit; they blindly read 2 or 4 bytes at the supplied address. Safety is therefore a whole-function contract: EVERY call site must pre-validate that the pointer has enough bytes. Auditing the sites here (exif at 551/560/565 guarded by length>=16; directory at 595 guarded by 590; q and q+2/q+4/q+8 guarded by 610; directory+2+12*number_entries at 842 guarded by 840; p at 814 as analyzed above) shows all are currently bounded. The latent risk (CWE-125) lives in these helpers' implementations (file not provided) and in the fragility of that non-local contract: a single new or moved ReadProperty* call without a matching bound check reads past the EXIF blob.

number_entries is an attacker-controlled 16-bit count (up to 65535) and q is computed as directory+12*entry+2. The per-iteration guard 'q > exif+length-12' correctly stops the loop when an entry would exceed the buffer, so the entry reads are bounded. The residual concern is pointer-arithmetic overflow: 12*entry and directory+... are computed before the comparison, and directory itself is exif+attacker_offset. For a maliciously small buffer near the top of the address space the addition directory+12*entry+2 could in theory wrap, defeating the '> exif+length-12' comparison and yielding an out-of-bounds read (CWE-190 leading to CWE-125). In practice 12*65535 (~786 KB) against a heap buffer makes this infeasible, so this is low severity, but the safe pattern is to bound 'entry' against a computed max-entries value rather than rely on pointer comparison after the add.

After the format loop, the macros strip the trailing ', ' with buffer[len-2]='\0'. len is size_t and is clamped to at most MagickPathExtent-1, and the 'len > 1' guard ensures len-2 does not underflow to a huge index (len==0 or 1 skips the write). So the write index is in [0, MagickPathExtent-3] and is safe. This is called out only to dismiss it: there is no off-by-one or unsigned-underflow write here despite the size_t subtraction, because the len>1 precondition and the MagickPathExtent-1 clamp jointly bound it.

summary: This is a post-fix revision of ImageMagick's GetEXIFProperty EXIF/TIFF parser and it is well hardened: every attacker-controlled offset, entry count, format code, and component count is range-checked, the IFD-recursion stack is depth-limited, a SplayTree and duplicate scan break parse cycles, and the number_bytes span checks keep value reads inside the blob. No directly exploitable memory-safety bug was found in the code shown. The residual risks are latent and contractual: the value-extraction macros perform no local bounds check and rely on a non-local invariant (size==tag_bytes[format] plus the span checks), the pre-value guard at line 652 validates only one element rather than the full span, the description[36] table is exactly saturated by its longest literal, and the ReadProperty* helpers take pointers with no length so safety depends on every call site's guard staying correct.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Fixed-size stack indexed by 'level' with multi-step increments under a level<MaxDirectoryStack-2 guard; must confirm index stays in [0,15].
Unused delimiter constant in the shown code; no data flow, no security contract.
Documentary count of format types; the actual index bound comes from sizeof(tag_bytes)/sizeof(*tag_bytes) at line 617.
Switch/index labels; format is range-checked to [1,12] at lines 617-620 before use as tag_bytes[] index.
These tags make the entry value a sub-IFD pointer, driving the recursive stack push and re-parse; covered by the stack-bound finding and the offset-read note.
Loops 'components' times reading p1 with no local bound; safety is non-local (relies on size==tag_bytes[format] and number_bytes span checks).
Same unbounded p1 read pattern (reads p1 and p1+4 per component); folded into the macro over-read finding.
size_t subtraction, but len>1 guard and MagickPathExtent-1 clamp make it safe; documented as dismissed-in-detail.
Internal stack bookkeeping; each field is validated on pop (directory re-checked at 590, offset used as tag_offset base only).
Longest literal is exactly 35+NUL=36 bytes; zero headroom, NUL-termination assumed by LocaleCompare/FormatLocaleString.
Read-only table key; compared for equality, not used in arithmetic against buffers.
Static const lookup data; loops terminate on tag==0 sentinel; not attacker-writable.
Indexed only by 'format' after the [1,12] range check at 617-620; index is bounded.
All writes go through FormatLocaleString bounded by MagickPathExtent-len; write side cannot overflow.
Each entry access guarded by q>exif+length-12 (610); pointer-arith overflow noted as low-risk finding.
Rejected if negative (622) and product overflow caught by number_bytes<components (625).
Overflow-guarded at 625; bounded to <=4 inline or <=length via dir_offset checks (638-643).
Range-checked to [1,12] at 617-620 before indexing tag_bytes[] and the switch.
Each range-checked (<0 and >=length) before forming a pointer (566, 638-643, 815, 844).
Validates only one element, not the full number_bytes span; insufficient alone though currently backed by span checks.
Correctly checks the full span before the copy; this is the safe pattern.
Correctly bounds the IFD pointer before the 2-byte number_entries read.
Bounds the 4-byte next-IFD-link read at 842 correctly.
Multi-byte readers take a pointer with no length; safety is a per-call-site contract, all sites bounded here but fragile/latent.
property is caller/API input, validated at 443-448; the untrusted surface is the image profile bytes, not the signature.
Anti-cycle guard; keyed on entry pointers q, break on revisit (612); reduces DoS/loop risk rather than introducing one.

findings.title: Value-extraction macros read 'components' elements from p1 with no local bounds check (non-local safety invariant)
findings.anchor: EXIFMultipleValues / EXIFMultipleFractions loop: for (component=0; component < components; component++) { ... p1+=size; }
findings.code:
for (component=0; component < components; component++) \
   { \
     len+=FormatLocaleString(buffer+len,MagickPathExtent-len,format", ",arg); \
     if (len >= (MagickPathExtent-1)) \
       len=MagickPathExtent-1; \
     p1+=size; \
   } \
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read (unbounded loop read over attacker element count)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/eval/eval_0025_cwe703.c (macros at 24-47 and 49-73, expanded at cases 677-726)
findings.invariant: For every format case, p (the loop's start p1) through p + components*size must lie within [exif, exif+length]. This holds only because size passed to the macro equals tag_bytes[format] and number_bytes=components*tag_bytes[format] is bounded (<=4 inline at 627, or <=length via the dir_offset checks at 638-643). The macro itself enforces nothing.
findings.failure_mode: The loop reads components elements (2/4/8 bytes each; fractions read p1 and p1+4) advancing p1 by 'size' with no per-iteration comparison against exif+length. In this fixed revision the reads stay in-bounds, but if any case is added/edited with size != tag_bytes[format], or the number_bytes span checks are weakened, an attacker-supplied large component count reads past the EXIF heap allocation and the out-of-bounds bytes are formatted into the returned property string (information disclosure / crash).
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm for every EXIFMultipleValues/EXIFMultipleFractions call site that the macro 'size' argument exactly equals tag_bytes[format] for that case, and that number_bytes (=components*size) is always bounded by the inline<=4 or dir_offset+number_bytes<=length checks before the switch is reached.

findings.title: Pre-value bounds check validates a single element, not the full number_bytes span
findings.bug_class: Insufficient bounds check (checks one element instead of the consumed span)
findings.invariant: Before reading a multi-element value, the code must validate p + number_bytes <= exif+length (the full span the loop will read), not merely p + tag_bytes[format] (one element).
findings.failure_mode: This guard only ensures one element (tag_bytes[format] bytes) is readable at p. For components>1 the extraction loop reads number_bytes >> tag_bytes[format]. The over-read is currently prevented by the separate number_bytes span checks at 627/642, so this line is redundant rather than exploitable; but as the sole guard it would not catch a regression in the span logic. The STRING branch at 731 already uses the correct full-span form (exif+length-number_bytes).
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify that the number_bytes span checks at 627 (inline <=4) and 638-643 (dir_offset+number_bytes<=length) always execute before this point for every non-STRING format, so that the single-element check here is never the only bound.

findings.title: IFD-recursion stack directory_stack[16] indexed by a bare 'level' counter with multi-step increments
findings.anchor: directory_stack[level] writes under guard 'level < (MaxDirectoryStack-2)' with multiple level++
findings.bug_class: Potential out-of-bounds stack write (fixed array + counter)
findings.invariant: level must remain in [0,15] across every directory_stack[level] write. With entry guarded by level<14 and at most two subsequent level++ before re-guarding, the maximum written index is 14 — within bounds.
findings.failure_mode: Each EXIF/GPS/Interop offset tag pushes 1-2 frames and increments level up to three times. The code is safe as written (max index 14 for a [16] array, inner push at 847-850 re-guarded by level<14). The risk is fragility: changing the guard bound (e.g. to MaxDirectoryStack-1) or adding another unconditional level++ would let directory_stack[level] index >=16, an out-of-bounds write of an attacker-controlled directory pointer onto the stack.
findings.what_to_confirm: Trace all level++ statements (827, 839, 850) against the level<MaxDirectoryStack-2 guards to confirm no path writes directory_stack[level] with level>=MaxDirectoryStack, and that every push is preceded by a fresh level bound check.

findings.title: TagInfo.description[36] exactly saturated by its longest literal (no NUL headroom)
findings.bug_class: Fixed buffer sized with zero headroom; loss of NUL termination on future edit
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/eval/eval_0025_cwe703.c line 93 (struct); table at 99-395; consumers at 518 (LocaleCompare) and 774 (FormatLocaleString %s)
findings.invariant: Every EXIFTag[] description string literal must be at most 35 characters so the terminating NUL fits inside description[36]; the array must always be a valid NUL-terminated C string for LocaleCompare and FormatLocaleString.
findings.failure_mode: The longest current literal, 'exif:ThumbnailPrimaryChromaticities' (line 227), is exactly 35 chars + NUL = 36 bytes. C allows initializing char[36] from a 36-char literal by dropping the NUL. If any tag name of length >=36 is ever added, that entry is stored without a terminator, and the subsequent LocaleCompare (518) or %s FormatLocaleString (774) reads past the struct until it finds a stray zero byte (out-of-bounds read / info leak).
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm no description literal exceeds 35 characters (or enlarge the array / use a pointer), and that the compiler is not silently accepting an initializer that drops the NUL.

findings.title: ReadProperty* multi-byte readers take a pointer with no length; safety is a per-call-site contract
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) for the readers; call sites in corpus/eval/eval_0025_cwe703.c (551, 560, 565, 595, 615, 616, 621, 637, 814, 842)
findings.invariant: Each multi-byte ReadProperty* call site must independently guarantee the pointer has 2 or 4 readable bytes within [exif, exif+length] before the call, because the reader receives no length and cannot bound itself.
findings.failure_mode: The readers blindly dereference 2-4 bytes at the given address. Every call site in this function is currently guarded (length>=16 for the header reads, directory<=exif+length-2 at 590, q<=exif+length-12 at 610, the 840 guard for the next-IFD read, and the pointer arithmetic bounding p for the 814 read). The latent hazard is that a single future ReadProperty* call added or moved without a matching bound check reads past the EXIF blob; the contract is easy to violate because it is invisible in the reader's signature.
findings.what_to_confirm: Audit every ReadProperty* call to confirm a preceding bound check covers its byte width, and consider a length-aware reader variant to make the contract local.

audit_checklist: For each EXIFMultipleValues/EXIFMultipleFractions case, confirm the macro 'size' argument equals tag_bytes[format], so components*size == number_bytes and the p1 loop stays within the span already bounded at lines 627/638-643.

Verify number_bytes is bounded on BOTH paths before any value read: inline path requires number_bytes<=4 (627) and offset path requires dir_offset>=0, dir_offset<length, and dir_offset+number_bytes<=length without wrap (638-643).

Trace directory_stack pushes: confirm every write to directory_stack[level] is preceded by a level<MaxDirectoryStack-2 guard and that the up-to-three level++ steps can never index >=MaxDirectoryStack (currently max index 14).
Confirm the pre-value guard at 652 is never the sole bound for a multi-element read; the full-span checks (627/642) or the STRING-branch check at 731 must cover number_bytes.
Confirm no EXIFTag[] description literal exceeds 35 characters so the NUL fits in description[36]; ideally enlarge the array to remove the zero-headroom trap.
Verify every multi-byte ReadProperty* call site pre-validates the pointer has its 2/4 bytes inside [exif, exif+length]; check the readers' implementation does not read beyond the pointer.
Confirm the SplayTree revisit check (612) and duplicate-directory scan (831-835) fully prevent infinite re-parse loops across chained/next-IFD links (840-850).
Confirm format is always validated to [1,12] (617-620) before indexing tag_bytes[format] and dispatching the value switch, and that format==0 is rejected.
Confirm the 'components<0' (622) and 'number_bytes<components' overflow (625) guards fire before number_bytes is used, and that no signed/unsigned confusion lets number_bytes go negative when cast.
